q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><msqrt><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mi>x</mi><msqrt><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></mrow></munder><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><msqrt><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mi>x</mi><msqrt><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mo>∑</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi>S</mi></mrow></munder><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
Given,

sin-1x+1x2+2x+2-sin-1xx2+1=π4

⇒sin-1x+1x2+x+2=π4+sin-1xx2+1

⇒x+1x2+x+2=sinπ4+sin-1xx2+1

⇒x+1x2+x+2=12×1x2+1+12×xx2+1

⇒x+1x2+x+2=x+12x2+1

⇒x+12x2+1-x2+x+2=0

⇒x=-1 or x2+x+2=2·x2+1

Now solving, x2+x+2=2·x2+1 we get,

x2+x+2=2x2+1

⇒x2-x=0

⇒x=0, x=1 {rejected as x=1 will not satisfy the given equation}

Hence, S=0,1

Now solving,

∑n∈Ssinx2+x+5π2-cosx2+x+5π

=sin5π2-cos5π+sin5π2-cos5π

=1--1+1--1=4

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of solutions of $\tan^{-1} 4x + \tan^{-1} 6x = \frac{\pi}{6}$, where $-\frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}} < x < \frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}$, is equal to
question: The number of solutions of $\tan^{-1} 4x + \tan^{-1} 6x = \frac{\pi}{6}$, where $-\frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}} < x < \frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}$, is equal to
solution:
For $x \in \left(-\frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}, \frac{1}{2\sqrt{6}}\right)$, $24x^2 < 1$
So $\tan^{-1}(4x) + \tan^{-1}(6x) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{10x}{1-24x^2}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6}$.
$\frac{10x}{1-24x^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \Rightarrow 24x^2 + 10\sqrt{3}x - 1 = 0$.
$x = \frac{-10\sqrt{3} \pm \sqrt{300+96}}{48} = \frac{-5\sqrt{3} \pm 3\sqrt{11}}{24}$.
$x_1 = \frac{-5\sqrt{3}+3\sqrt{11}}{24} \approx 0.054$ (valid, lies in range).
$x_2 = \frac{-5\sqrt{3}-3\sqrt{11}}{24} \approx -0.776$ (rejected, outside range).
Number of solutions $= 1$.

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> represent a parabola with focus <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and directrix <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></msqrt><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><msqrt><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo></math>

Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="}" open="{"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi><mo>:</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msqrt><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></msqrt><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><msqrt><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></msqrt></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo></math>
solution:
Equation of parabola with focus (-12, 0) and directrix y=-12 is:

x+122=y+14

⇒y=x2+x

Put f(x)=y=x+x2 in S,

tan-1xx+1+sin-1x2+x+1=π2

⇒0≤x2+x+1≤1

⇒x2+x≤0      …1

Also,

 x2+x≥0           …2

By 1 & 2, we have

x2+x=0⇒x=0,-1

S contains 2 element.

subject: Mathematics
q: If $y = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3\cos x - 4\sin x}{4\cos x + 3\sin x}\right) + 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{1+\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)$, then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at $x = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ is equal to:
question: If $y = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3\cos x - 4\sin x}{4\cos x + 3\sin x}\right) + 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{1+\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)$, then $\dfrac{dy}{dx}$ at $x = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ is equal to:
solution:
Let $y = y_1 + y_2$

$y_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3\cos x - 4\sin x}{4\cos x + 3\sin x}\right)$

Dividing the numerator and denominator by $4\cos x$:

$y_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\dfrac{3}{4} - \tan x}{1 + \dfrac{3}{4}\tan x}\right)$

Using the formula $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{A - B}{1 + AB}\right) = \tan^{-1}A - \tan^{-1}B$:

$y_1 = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) - \tan^{-1}(\tan x) = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{3}{4}\right) - x$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$\dfrac{dy_1}{dx} = -1$

Now, $y_2 = 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x}{1+\sqrt{1-x^2}}\right)$

Substituting $x = \sin \theta$:

$y_2 = 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\sin \theta}{1+\cos \theta}\right)$

$y_2 = 2\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2\sin(\theta/2)\cos(\theta/2)}{2\cos^2(\theta/2)}\right)$

$y_2 = 2\tan^{-1}\left(\tan\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = 2\left(\dfrac{\theta}{2}\right) = \theta = \sin^{-1}x$

Differentiating with respect to $x$:

$\dfrac{dy_2}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}$

At $x = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$:

$\dfrac{dy_2}{dx} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \left(\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{3}{4}}} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{4}}} = 2$

Therefore, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{dy_1}{dx} + \dfrac{dy_2}{dx} = -1 + 2 = 1$

Answer: $1$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\cos ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} \frac{y}{2}=\alpha$, then $4 x^2-4 x y \cos \alpha+y^2$ is equal to
question: If $\cos ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} \frac{y}{2}=\alpha$, then $4 x^2-4 x y \cos \alpha+y^2$ is equal to
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \cos ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} \frac{y}{2}=\alpha \\
& \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{x y}{2}+\sqrt{\left(-x^2\right)\left(1-\frac{y^2}{4}\right)}\right)=\alpha \\
& \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{x y+\sqrt{4-y^2-4 x^2+x^2 y^2}}{2}\right)=\alpha \\
& \Rightarrow 4-y^2-4 x^2+x^2 y^2=4 \cos ^2 \alpha+x^2 y^2-4 x y \cos \alpha \\
& \Rightarrow 4 x^2+y^2-4 x y \cos \alpha=4 \sin ^2 \alpha
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $[\cdot]$ denote the greatest integer function. If the domain of the function $f(x) = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x+[x]}{3}\right)$ is $[\alpha, \beta)$, then $\alpha^2 + \beta^2$ is equal to:
question: Let $[\cdot]$ denote the greatest integer function. If the domain of the function $f(x) = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x+[x]}{3}\right)$ is $[\alpha, \beta)$, then $\alpha^2 + \beta^2$ is equal to:
solution:
For the function $f(x) = \sin^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x+[x]}{3}\right)$ to be defined, the argument of the inverse sine function must lie in the interval $[-1, 1]$.

$-1 \le \dfrac{x+[x]}{3} \le 1$

$-3 \le x + [x] \le 3$

Using the fractional part function $\{x\}$, we can write $x = [x] + \{x\}$, where $0 \le \{x\} < 1$. Substituting this into the inequality gives:

$-3 \le 2[x] + \{x\} \le 3$

We analyze the possible integer values for $[x]$:

If $[x] \le -2$, then $2[x] + \{x\} \le -4 + \{x\} < -3$, which does not satisfy the inequality.

If $[x] = -1$, then $2[x] + \{x\} = -2 + \{x\}$. Since $0 \le \{x\} < 1$, we have $-2 \le -2 + \{x\} < -1$, which satisfies the inequality. This gives $x \in [-1, 0)$.

If $[x] = 0$, then $2[x] + \{x\} = \{x\}$. Since $0 \le \{x\} < 1$, this satisfies the inequality. This gives $x \in [0, 1)$.

If $[x] = 1$, then $2[x] + \{x\} = 2 + \{x\}$. Since $0 \le \{x\} < 1$, we have $2 \le 2 + \{x\} < 3$, which satisfies the inequality. This gives $x \in [1, 2)$.

If $[x] \ge 2$, then $2[x] + \{x\} \ge 4 + \{x\} \ge 4 > 3$, which does not satisfy the inequality.

Taking the union of the valid intervals, the domain of $f(x)$ is $[-1, 0) \cup [0, 1) \cup [1, 2) = [-1, 2)$.

Comparing this with the given domain $[\alpha, \beta)$, we get $\alpha = -1$ and $\beta = 2$.

Therefore, $\alpha^2 + \beta^2 = (-1)^2 + (2)^2 = 1 + 4 = 5$.

Answer: $5$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the maximum value of $\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}+\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)^{2}$ for $x \in\left[-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$ be $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}} \pi^{2}$, where $\operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$. Then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
question: Let the maximum value of $\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)^{2}+\left(\cos ^{-1} x\right)^{2}$ for $x \in\left[-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]$ be $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{n}} \pi^{2}$, where $\operatorname{gcd}(\mathrm{m}, \mathrm{n})=1$. Then $\mathrm{m}+\mathrm{n}$ is equal to $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Let $\alpha = \sin^{-1}x$ and $\beta = \cos^{-1}x$. Using $\alpha + \beta = \frac{\pi}{2}$:
$f(x) = \alpha^2 + \beta^2 = \alpha^2 + (\frac{\pi}{2} - \alpha)^2 = 2\alpha^2 - \pi\alpha + \frac{\pi^2}{4}$
This equals $2(\alpha - \frac{\pi}{4})^2 + \frac{\pi^2}{8}$, which is minimized at $\alpha = \frac{\pi}{4}$.
For $x \in [-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}]$, we have $\alpha \in [-\frac{\pi}{3}, \frac{\pi}{4}]$.
Distance from $-\frac{\pi}{3}$ to vertex: $\frac{\pi}{4} + \frac{\pi}{3} = \frac{7\pi}{12}$. Distance from $\frac{\pi}{4}$ to vertex: 0.
Maximum occurs at $x = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ where $\alpha = -\frac{\pi}{3}$:
$f(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}) = 2(\frac{\pi^2}{9}) + \frac{\pi^2}{3} + \frac{\pi^2}{4} = \pi^2(\frac{8 + 12 + 9}{36}) = \frac{29}{36}\pi^2$
Since $\gcd(29, 36) = 1$, we have $m = 29, n = 36$, so $m + n = 65$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2^{p-1}}{1 + 2^{2p-1}}\right) = \alpha$, then $\tan\alpha$ is equal to __________.
question: If $\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2^{p-1}}{1 + 2^{2p-1}}\right) = \alpha$, then $\tan\alpha$ is equal to __________.
solution:
The general term of the given series is $T_p = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2^{p-1}}{1 + 2^{2p-1}}\right)$.

This can be rewritten by expressing the numerator and denominator in terms of $2^p$ and $2^{p-1}$:

$T_p = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2^p - 2^{p-1}}{1 + 2^p \cdot 2^{p-1}}\right)$

Using the inverse trigonometric identity $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x - y}{1 + xy}\right) = \tan^{-1}x - \tan^{-1}y$, we get:

$T_p = \tan^{-1}(2^p) - \tan^{-1}(2^{p-1})$

Summing $T_p$ from $p = 1$ to $11$ gives a telescoping series:

$\displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} T_p = \left(\tan^{-1}(2^1) - \tan^{-1}(2^0)\right) + \left(\tan^{-1}(2^2) - \tan^{-1}(2^1)\right) + \dots + \left(\tan^{-1}(2^{11}) - \tan^{-1}(2^{10})\right)$

Canceling the intermediate terms, we obtain:

$\displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} T_p = \tan^{-1}(2^{11}) - \tan^{-1}(2^0)$

Since $2^0 = 1$ and $\tan^{-1}(1) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$, the sum becomes:

$\displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} T_p = \tan^{-1}(2^{11}) - \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

Substituting this back into the expression for $\alpha$:

$\alpha = \dfrac{\pi}{4} + \tan^{-1}(2^{11}) - \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(2^{11})$

Taking the tangent of both sides, we get:

$\tan\alpha = 2^{11} = 2048$

Answer: $2048$

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{ll}a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22}\end{array}\right): a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{22}\right\}$
Then the number of non-singular matrices in the set $S$ is :
question:
Let $S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{ll}a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22}\end{array}\right): a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{22}\right\}$
Then the number of non-singular matrices in the set $S$ is :
solution:
The matrices in the form
$$
\left[\begin{array}{ll}
a_{11} & a_{12} \\
a_{21} & a_{22}
\end{array}\right], a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{12} \text { are }
$$
$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 1
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
2 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
$$
At any place, $0 / 1 / 2$ means 0,1 or 2 will be the element at that place.

Hence there are total $27=3 \times 3+3 \times 3+3 \times 3$ ) matrices of the above form. Out of which the matrices which are singular are
$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 \\
1 / 2 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{ll}
1 & 1 \\
1 & 1
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{ll}
2 & 2 \\
2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
$$
Hence there are total $7(=3+2+1+1)$ singular matrices.

Therefore number of all non-singular matrices in the given form $=27-7=20$

Given, sin-1sinθ-cos-1sinθ>0,θ∈0,2π

∵sin-1θ+cos-1θ=π2

∴sin-1sinθ-π2-sin-1sinθ>0

sin-1sinθ>π4⇒θ∈π4,3π4

a,b=π4,3π4⇒b-a=π2

Given b-a=α-β

∴ α-β=π2  .....(1)

Now αx2+βx+sin-1x2-6x+10+cos-1x2-6x+10=0

Now defining x2-6x+10=1+x-32≥1

Hence x=3 is the only possible solution

9α+3β+π2=0       …2

On solving equations 1 and 2 we get,

α=π12

solution:
Given equation is:

cos-12x-2cos-11-x2=π,x∈-12,12

It is possible if

cos-12x=π    ....(1)

and  2cos-11-x2=0    ....(2)

From equation (1), x=-12 which is not satisfying equation (2).

So, no such x exists.
